context:
## hev ask

hev ask (`@hev/ask`) is a `⌘K` search overlay for **Astro docs sites**, shipped as an Astro integration. A site adds `hevAsk()` to its config, drops the `SearchOverlay` component in a layout, and gets two search paths over its content collections:

- **Keyword search** — instant and keyless: token-overlap matching over heading-level chunks, widened by a glossary, deep-linking to `/docs/page#anchor`.
- **Agentic search** — on Enter, with an `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`: a bounded Claude tool-use loop that reads the committed **ask digest** progressively and streams a grounded answer (SSE) with inline deep links to real doc sections.

The **ask digest** (`.hev-ask/digest.json`) is the core artifact: a committed, offline-built, reviewable JSON distillation of the docs — section summaries, verbatim facts, a glossary, and an overview map. It was called the knowledge graph (kg) in earlier versions, and users may still use that name. It is built with the `ask` CLI (`ask digest build`, or the keyless sharded skill flow: `corpus` → distil → `assemble`) and gated by a content hash so unchanged docs never spend model work. `ask digest verify` checks every anchor, coverage, and literal fidelity in CI.

Anchors come from `github-slugger`, matching Astro's rendered heading ids exactly — that is why deep links stay trustworthy. Everything degrades instead of failing: no API key → keyword mode; no digest → raw keyword search. The endpoint (`POST /api/ask`) renders on demand, so sites need a server or hybrid adapter. The `ask` CLI and a stdio MCP server expose the same read surface (glossary, sections, overview, search, answer) to agents and CI.

Readers are typically Astro docs-site authors evaluating hev ask against Pagefind, Algolia, or Orama, asking: how does it work, what does it cost, what are the limits and tradeoffs, and how do I add it in five minutes.

glossary.definition: The committed, offline-built .hev-ask/digest.json artifact distilling the docs into section nodes, glossary, and overview that the agentic loop reads.
The bounded Claude tool-use loop run on Enter that gathers sections then streams a grounded answer.
The keyless, debounced token-overlap search over heading chunks, widened by the glossary.
A heading-bounded slice of a doc with its own URL anchor; the unit hev ask indexes and links to.
The github-slugger heading id appended to a page URL so results land on the exact section.
Digest terms with aliases and definitions that expand keyword queries before retrieval.
The on-demand /api/ask route serving suggestions, digest reads, keyword JSON, and agentic SSE.
The Astro component rendering the ⌘K search palette dropped into a layout.
The stdio server (ask mcp) exposing digest reads and answer as tools for coding agents.
The content-hash check that skips the digest model call when the corpus is unchanged.

suggestions: How do I add hev ask to my Astro site?
What happens if I don't set an ANTHROPIC_API_KEY?
How do I rebuild the ask digest after editing my docs?
How is this different from Pagefind or Algolia?
How do deep links stay correct when headings change?

nodes.title: CLI
nodes.summary: The ask CLI reads, builds, verifies, and serves the committed digest for agents and CI. It splits into consumer read verbs that progressively disclose the digest and producer commands under a digest group for building, emitting a corpus, assembling, verifying, and reporting status.

nodes.title: CLI
nodes.heading: Building the digest
nodes.summary: Producer commands run from the site root: a hash-gated one-shot build that calls the model only when the corpus is stale, plus a corpus/assemble pair for keyless skill-driven builds. The one-shot build is bounded and fails past a size limit, directing you to the sharded flow; in all cases the model authors only the distillation while structure, facts, and hashes are computed in code.

facts.literal:
ask digest build        # one-shot build; needs ANTHROPIC_API_KEY only when stale
ask digest corpus       # emit sections for a keyless skill/model distillation
ask digest assemble     # assemble .hev-ask/digest.json from that distillation
ask digest verify       # build the site and verify anchors, coverage, fidelity
ask digest status       # report shard coverage for a sharded build

nodes.title: CLI
nodes.heading: Claude Code skill
nodes.summary: The bundled build-digest skill builds the digest through the deterministic producer seam without spending tokens on your own API key, since the model step runs inside your Claude Code subscription. It runs sharded with one fresh-context distillation per shard so corpus size never hits a context limit, and its output matches the one-shot build's shape and obeys the same hash gate.

facts.literal:
ask digest corpus --shards-dir .hev-ask/shards    -> input-<id>.json + manifest.json
...one fresh-context distillation per shard       -> distill-<id>.json...
...one synthesis pass over the shard notes        -> global.json...
ask digest assemble --input-dir .hev-ask/shards   -> .hev-ask/digest.json

nodes.title: CLI
nodes.heading: Distribution
nodes.summary: The npm package exposes a single bin whose launcher resolves an env override first, then a platform-specific optional binary package, then the checked-out Go source. The source fallback is for development; published installs use the packaged binary.

nodes.title: CLI
nodes.heading: Flags
nodes.summary: Lists the CLI flags with defaults and descriptions, covering digest and endpoint paths, output formatting, result caps, collection and chunking options, model selection, sharding, and the verify build controls. Global flags precede the command name.

facts.literal:
ask --digest-path .hev-ask/digest.json --json search "openapi"
ask --endpoint https://askhev.com/api/ask mcp
ask digest build --collection docs --collection guides --chunk-heading-depth 2
ask digest verify --skip-build

nodes.title: CLI
nodes.heading: Go library
nodes.summary: A reusable Go API lets you call pure digest helpers directly or mount the dependency-free command group inside your own CLI. Lower-level helpers cover loading, listing, getting, searching, endpoint clients, building, anchor verification, and serving MCP.

nodes.title: CLI
nodes.heading: MCP
nodes.summary: The CLI can run a stdio MCP server over the same read and search surface, configurable to read a local digest for checked-out repos or a deployed endpoint when the agent needs the remote digest or answer. Tool schemas and behavior are detailed on the MCP page.

nodes.title: CLI
nodes.heading: Package scripts
nodes.summary: Shows wiring the producer commands into a site's package scripts so building and verifying the digest become standard npm scripts.

nodes.title: CLI
nodes.heading: Reading the digest
nodes.summary: By default the CLI reads the local committed digest from the current repo, or pass an endpoint flag to read a deployed site's HTTP API instead. The answer verb requires an endpoint because it uses the deployed agentic SSE path, while local retrieval uses the keyless search verb.

facts.literal:
ask glossary list
ask glossary get "ask digest"
ask sections list --group API
ask section get api/endpoint#digest-reads-get
ask overview
ask search "read endpoints"
ask --endpoint https://askhev.com/api/ask answer "what read routes exist?"
ask mcp

nodes.title: CLI
nodes.heading: Sharded builds for large sites
nodes.summary: Sharded builds remove the single-context size bound by splitting the corpus along slug-prefix boundaries, distilling each shard independently in a fresh context, and merging on assembly. Sharding is stable and incremental so editing one doc re-pends only its shard; stale distillations are detected and skipped while affected sections fall back to plain excerpts, and verify checks anchors, coverage, and fidelity against the committed digest.

facts.literal:
ask digest corpus --shards-dir .hev-ask/shards   # input-<id>.json per shard + manifest.json
ask digest status --shards-dir .hev-ask/shards   # distilled / pending / stale, per shard
# ...one distillation per shard writes distill-<id>.json; a final pass writes global.json...
ask digest assemble --input-dir .hev-ask/shards  # merge + write .hev-ask/digest.json

nodes.title: CLI
nodes.heading: Where it runs
nodes.summary: Producer commands run locally or in CI with filesystem access, and the Astro integration also invokes the build during astro build when a key is present, falling back to the committed artifact otherwise. The deployed site reads the committed digest through a virtual module and needs no filesystem access; running verify on every build keeps generated anchors matching Astro's rendered output.

nodes.title: Configuration
nodes.summary: The hevAsk() integration is the default export and takes one options object in which only collections is effectively required and everything else has a default. Options span collections, models, endpoint and base path, chunking depth, retrieval caps, and digest paths.

nodes.title: Configuration
nodes.heading: Options
nodes.summary: Documents every integration option with its type, default, and effect, including the indexed collections, URL base path, endpoint route, the loop and digest model choices, result and grounding caps, token and iteration budgets, chunk heading depth, retrieval candidate caps, and the digest path and content globs. Changing the endpoint requires passing the same value to the overlay's matching prop.

nodes.title: Configuration
nodes.heading: Tuning notes
nodes.summary: Practical guidance on tuning the knobs: chunk heading depth trades anchor granularity against section size, iteration count trades latency against multi-part recall, the per-document cap controls result spread, and the per-search candidate count trades recall against token cost.

nodes.title: Configuration
nodes.heading: TypeScript
nodes.summary: Notes that the integration's options type is exported for editor assistance and config typing.

nodes.title: Configuration
nodes.heading: What the integration does
nodes.summary: At config setup the integration injects the on-demand endpoint route, registers the config and digest virtual modules, watches the digest path for dev reloads, and warns if no collections are set. At build start it runs the hash-gated digest build when a key is present and otherwise warns and proceeds with the committed artifact, never failing for a missing key.

nodes.title: Digest format
nodes.summary: The committed digest.json is the agent's distilled, source-grounded view of the docs, built offline and inlined into the build through a virtual module so the running site reads it without filesystem access. Every node links back to its source page by anchor; humans read the rendered pages while the loop reads the digest and links back. It was formerly called the knowledge graph or kg.

nodes.title: Digest format
nodes.heading: Degradation
nodes.summary: The digest is read defensively: a missing, malformed, or older node-less file is used as-is, with the loop falling back to keyword-style search and nothing hard-failing. Being committed JSON, its deterministic structure is reviewable in pull requests while only the distillation is model-authored, which is why it lives as a file and why the model step can move into a Claude Code skill.

nodes.title: Digest format
nodes.heading: DigestNode
nodes.summary: Describes the per-section node shape: an id mapping one-to-one to a real anchor, the model's summary the agent reasons from, deterministically extracted verbatim facts the agent can quote without re-reading, source provenance, a mode marking reference sections as source-primary, and distinctive terms backing the render-time link check.

nodes.title: Digest format
nodes.heading: Fields
nodes.summary: Lists the top-level digest fields with their types and roles, including the schema version, build timestamp, content hash freshness gate, orientation context, glossary, deterministic section overview, model-authored suggestions, the section nodes that form the body, and a reserved empty edges array.

nodes.title: Digest format
nodes.heading: How each field is used
nodes.summary: Explains how each field feeds the system: overview and nodes are injected into the prompt-cached loop, the glossary expands keyword queries, nodes lift keyword ranking above incidental mentions, terms back a cited-link degradation check, suggestions populate the overlay, and the content hash gates regeneration.

nodes.title: Digest format
nodes.heading: Regenerating
nodes.summary: Rebuild and commit the digest after content changes; the hash gate makes this safe on every build by spending model work only when content actually changed. Build it with the keyless Claude Code skill or the single-call CLI build, with large sites using the sharded flow that re-distils only the shards an edit touches, then run verify to gate anchors, coverage, and literal fidelity.

nodes.title: Digest format
nodes.heading: Shape
nodes.summary: Shows a complete example of the digest JSON shape, illustrating the version, timestamp, content hash, context, glossary, overview, suggestions, a sample distilled node with its facts and sources, and the empty edges array.

nodes.title: Search endpoint
nodes.summary: The integration injects one on-demand route tree where the base route serves the overlay: keyword mode returns JSON and agentic mode streams a grounded answer as Server-Sent Events. Keyless sub-routes expose the committed digest for CLIs, MCP servers, and generated clients, with a published OpenAPI contract.

nodes.title: Search endpoint
nodes.heading: Agentic response (SSE)
nodes.summary: In agentic mode with a key present the endpoint streams a text/event-stream of named SSE frames: search frames report gathered context, a single sources frame sent before any token carries the grounding set clients validate links against, token frames deliver answer deltas, done ends the stream, and error reports a post-stream failure. Sources carry no snippet because the answer prose carries the substance.

nodes.title: Search endpoint
nodes.heading: Digest reads (GET)
nodes.summary: Several GET routes read the committed digest without any model call or API key, returning the glossary, a single glossary entry by term or alias, section summaries optionally filtered by group, a full node by id, and the overview plus context. Section ids containing slashes or hashes must be URL-encoded in the path, and missing terms, ids, or unknown routes return a 404 JSON error.

nodes.title: Search endpoint
nodes.heading: Errors
nodes.summary: Documents the endpoint's error responses: a 400 for invalid JSON bodies, a 404 for missing read routes, glossary terms, or section ids, and a 500 when the chunk index fails to build. Failures during the agentic stream arrive as a final SSE error event because the HTTP status is already 200.

nodes.title: Search endpoint
nodes.heading: Index lifecycle
nodes.summary: The chunk index is built once per server instance on the first request and cached for the process lifetime. On that first request the endpoint compares the live content hash against the digest's and logs a one-time warning on mismatch, cueing a rebuild.

nodes.title: Search endpoint
nodes.heading: Keyword response (JSON)
nodes.summary: Keyword mode returns a 200 JSON envelope of ranked results with title, optional heading, deep-linked URL, group, and snippet, alongside the echoed query, the configured loop model, and the mode. A warning field appears when agentic was requested but no key is configured, signaling the downgrade.

nodes.title: Search endpoint
nodes.heading: LLM tracing
nodes.summary: Setting a PostHog key in the environment makes every agentic answer emit an AI-generation trace capturing model, tokens, latency, and the loop's tool calls, with host and content-capture overrides available. Absent a key it is a no-op and the answer path never depends on telemetry.

nodes.title: Search endpoint
nodes.heading: Mode selection
nodes.summary: The endpoint selects what to run: empty queries return an empty keyword result, keyword mode or a missing key returns keyword JSON, and an agentic request without a key downgrades to keyword JSON plus a warning. There is no AI-unavailable error path; a missing key always degrades to results, and the overlay branches on the response content-type.

nodes.title: Search endpoint
nodes.heading: Request
nodes.summary: Requests are POSTed with a JSON body carrying the query and an optional mode. Keyword forces the instant path, agentic requests the loop, and an omitted mode behaves like agentic when a key is present, while an empty or whitespace query returns an empty result set.

nodes.title: Search endpoint
nodes.heading: Suggested questions (GET)
nodes.summary: A GET on the base route returns the digest's baked-in suggestions and the loop model with no query and no model call. The overlay fetches this once on first open when AI is enabled, and an empty suggestions array simply shows none.

nodes.title: Search endpoint
nodes.heading: The API key
nodes.summary: The endpoint resolves the Anthropic API key in order from the adapter runtime env, then the process env, then the build-time import env. It is read wherever the host injects server secrets and is never sent to the browser.

nodes.title: MCP server
nodes.summary: Running the MCP command starts a stdio Model Context Protocol server over the same digest reads as the CLI and HTTP API, giving coding agents a zero-glue path to structured doc tools. Point the agent at a checked-out repo's local digest or at a deployed endpoint.

nodes.title: MCP server
nodes.heading: Configure
nodes.summary: Shows MCP server configuration for both local keyless digest reads and a deployed site, where the endpoint form gives the freshest deployed digest and the answer tool. Local mode is fully offline and keyless, but the answer tool errors unless an endpoint is set.

nodes.title: MCP server
nodes.heading: Data sources
nodes.summary: The MCP server uses the same source resolution as the CLI: with an endpoint it calls the deployed read API and streams answers through the POST route, otherwise it reads the local digest path. Local reads reload the digest on each tool call so a just-rebuilt file is visible without restarting the server.

nodes.title: MCP server
nodes.heading: Protocol surface
nodes.summary: The server speaks newline-delimited JSON-RPC over stdio, handling initialization, tool listing, tool calls, and the initialized notification, with unknown methods returning a protocol error and tool failures returning an error result. All substantive behavior lives in the shared Go package so the CLI, command group, and MCP server reuse the same helpers.

nodes.title: MCP server
nodes.heading: Tools
nodes.summary: Lists the MCP tools with their arguments and returns, covering glossary listing and lookup, section listing and retrieval, overview, keyword search, and a streamed answer collapsed into one result. Each result includes human-readable text plus the original machine shape in structured content so agents can read or route it.

nodes.title: SearchOverlay component
nodes.summary: The SearchOverlay component renders the ⌘K command palette and is added once in a global layout. It opens over the page and does not affect layout until opened.

nodes.title: SearchOverlay component
nodes.heading: Keyboard model
nodes.summary: The overlay is ask-first and lets the number of typed words decide the path: suggested questions appear on open, one word runs debounced keyless keyword search with the first result auto-active, and typing a space switches to ask mode where Enter sends the question to the agentic loop. Enter opens the active keyword result in keyword mode, arrows move the selection, Esc closes, and without a server key asking returns keyword results with a surfaced warning.

nodes.title: SearchOverlay component
nodes.heading: Keyword results and deep links
nodes.summary: Each keyword result row shows the document title, an optional heading breadcrumb, and a one-line snippet, and its link is the chunk's URL which already carries the anchor. Clicking a result therefore lands on the exact heading.

nodes.title: SearchOverlay component
nodes.heading: Opening the overlay
nodes.summary: The overlay opens two built-in ways: a keyboard shortcut bound automatically once the component is on the page, and any element marked with the opener attribute opening it on click, so you can wire as many triggers as you like.

facts.literal: Search the docs

nodes.title: SearchOverlay component
nodes.heading: Props
nodes.summary: Documents the overlay's props with defaults: the endpoint it posts to, which must match the integration's endpoint option, the input placeholder text, and the debounce delay before a keyword query is sent.

nodes.title: SearchOverlay component
nodes.heading: Suggested questions
nodes.summary: When AI is enabled the overlay fetches a short list of suggested questions from the endpoint the first time it opens and shows them in the empty state. They come from the digest's baked-in suggestions so rendering them costs no model call, and clicking one fills the input and asks it immediately.

nodes.title: SearchOverlay component
nodes.heading: The mode toggle
nodes.summary: The overlay persists an AI-on-Enter preference in localStorage so readers can flip to keyword-only and never trigger a model call, where a space just searches a phrase and no suggested questions appear. The choice survives reloads.

nodes.title: SearchOverlay component
nodes.heading: The streamed answer
nodes.summary: Pressing Enter with a key configured replaces the keyword rows with an answer panel that first shows the model's live sub-queries, then streams the grounded answer token-by-token with a blinking caret. Inline deep links in accent color point at exact section anchors and a Sources row lists every section drawn from; links are validated against the streamed source set so any out-of-set link renders as plain text rather than a clickable dead link.

nodes.title: SearchOverlay component
nodes.heading: Theming
nodes.summary: The overlay's markup uses a class prefix and reads the page's CSS custom properties for background, text, muted text, and accent, so defining those tokens on the root makes the overlay inherit your palette. Because its scoped styles key off those variables, matching your look usually needs no overlay CSS overrides.

nodes.title: Concepts
nodes.summary: hev ask is three parts: a heading-chunk index with real anchors, an offline source-grounded digest of every section, and a bounded agentic loop that reads the digest progressively from primer to summaries to full source. The key split is that the digest is built offline with a strong model and committed to git while the index and loop run on demand at the edge, with no durable state in the running site.

nodes.title: Concepts
nodes.heading: Asking is the default
nodes.summary: The overlay is ask-first: a single word is an instant keyword lookup, but the moment the query grows past one word it switches to ask mode where Enter sends the question to the agentic loop. Suggested questions baked into the digest make asking the obvious move, yet a reader can flip to keyword-only and never call the model.

nodes.title: Concepts
nodes.heading: Chunks and anchors
nodes.summary: hev ask indexes sections rather than pages, splitting each document on its headings up to a configurable depth, with pre-heading content becoming an intro chunk URL'd to the page itself. Anchors are generated with the same slugger Astro's renderer uses so links land on headings that exist in the rendered HTML, and both the runtime index and offline build chunk through one shared function so their slugs agree.

nodes.title: Concepts
nodes.heading: Degradation, by design
nodes.summary: hev ask keeps working as pieces drop away: no key at runtime means keyword-only, no key at build keeps the committed digest with a non-fatal warning, a missing or node-less digest falls back to keyword-style retrieval and raw token-overlap ranking with no suggestions, and a stale digest logs a one-line warning but still serves. It points readers to Limits and Tradeoffs for the boundaries and the choices involved.

nodes.title: Concepts
nodes.heading: Keyword search and the glossary
nodes.summary: On a single term the server runs a dependency-free prefilter that expands the query with glossary aliases and matched-term tokens, scores by token overlap widened by the digest's summaries, terms, and facts, caps results per document, and excerpts around the first match for a snippet. This instant path needs no key or embeddings, and without a digest it degrades to plain token overlap over raw chunk text.

nodes.title: Concepts
nodes.heading: The agentic search loop
nodes.summary: When a reader asks with a key present, the query goes to a bounded tool-use loop with two phases: a gather phase where the model opens sections by id to read their summaries, verbatim facts, and reference source text up to a max number of rounds, then an answer phase where it is called once more with no tools so it can only write prose. Dropping the tools guarantees it answers, it may only cite sections it opened and link to provided URLs, and it cannot ground in what retrieval never found.

nodes.title: Concepts
nodes.heading: The ask digest
nodes.summary: The ask digest is the offline-built, committed, reviewable view of the docs holding distilled section nodes with summaries and verbatim facts plus source links, a deterministic overview, orientation context and glossary, and suggested questions. Only the summary, glossary, context, and suggestions are model-authored while the node structure, facts, overview, and content hash are derived in code, which is why the model step suits a Claude Code skill.

nodes.title: Concepts
nodes.heading: The system prompt is cached
nodes.summary: The digest's section map and summaries are injected into the system prompt with a cache marker so across rounds it is a prompt-cache hit rather than re-sent tokens. The answer turn changes the tool set so it cannot reuse the search rounds' cache, but it is the last call anyway, and the loop model defaults to Claude Haiku 4.5 and is configurable.

nodes.title: Concepts
nodes.heading: Two ways to build it
nodes.summary: The model step can run as a recommended Claude Code skill that walks Claude through reading the corpus and writing the distillation inside your subscription with no API key or per-build token spend, or as a fallback single-call CLI build using the default Opus model for CI or non-Claude-Code users. Either way the build is hash-gated so an unchanged corpus does no model work, and the resulting JSON is reviewed in pull requests.

nodes.title: Introduction
nodes.summary: hev ask distills an Astro docs site into a committed, token-efficient digest and serves it two ways: a ⌘K answer overlay for readers and an ask CLI plus MCP server so coding agents can query the same docs. You build the digest offline with the bundled skill and your Claude Code subscription, then serve single-turn grounded Q&A over your docs cheaply, suited to medium-sized corpora like technical docs and internal wikis.

nodes.title: Introduction
nodes.heading: Build it with your coding agent
nodes.summary: The digest is built inside your coding agent via the bundled Claude Code skill using your existing subscription, so there is no API key and no per-build token spend; you commit the JSON, drop the overlay into a layout, and get instant keyword results plus a Claude answer loop on Enter with every result deep-linked. hev ask reads only the configured Astro content collections with no crawler or external index, so the docs you render are the digest and the committed JSON is reviewable in every PR.

nodes.title: Introduction
nodes.heading: Next steps
nodes.summary: Points readers to the next pages: Quick start to add search fast, Concepts for chunks, anchors, the loop, and the digest, Tradeoffs and Limits for choices and boundaries, the CLI for building and querying, and the API reference for options, props, and the endpoint contract.

nodes.title: Introduction
nodes.heading: Who this is for
nodes.summary: hev ask is for those building or maintaining an Astro 5 docs site whose content lives in a Markdown or MDX content collection and who want out-of-the-box search and answers that deep-link to sections, understand questions phrased in the reader's words, and are queryable by coding agents. If you only need keyword search over a static site with no API key, Pagefind is suggested as a simpler fit, with an honest comparison in Tradeoffs.

nodes.title: Limits
nodes.summary: Lays out the hard boundaries to know before adopting hev ask, framing them as the edges of the current design rather than bugs.

nodes.title: Limits
nodes.heading: A server route is required
nodes.summary: The search endpoint renders on demand, so a fully static build cannot serve search and you need a server or hybrid adapter. Static-only sites should use a static search tool instead.

nodes.title: Limits
nodes.heading: Agentic search adds latency
nodes.summary: The agentic path is bounded by a configurable number of Claude round-trips and is a few seconds in the worst case, so it is not instant by nature. The keyword path is the always-available instant lane, and the iteration knob tightens the ceiling.

nodes.title: Limits
nodes.heading: Anchors depend on Astro's slugger
nodes.summary: Deep links are correct only while hev ask's generated heading slugs match Astro's rendered ids, which it keeps aligned by using the same slugger and a verify command that fails if any chunk anchor is missing from the built HTML. Wiring verify into CI catches any future slugging change before a broken link ships.

nodes.title: Limits
nodes.heading: Frontmatter parsing is a flat-YAML subset
nodes.summary: The offline build parses frontmatter with a small flat-YAML splitter rather than a full parser, handling common string and number fields but not nested structures. This affects only the offline build reading files from disk; the runtime index uses Astro's own collection loader, which honors your real schema.

nodes.title: Limits
nodes.heading: Recall has a keyword ceiling
nodes.summary: Retrieval is glossary-widened keyword token-overlap rather than embeddings, and the agentic loop can only ground in what retrieval surfaces. The glossary recovers most synonym cases, but a query sharing no tokens with the docs and uncovered by the glossary may never reach the candidate pool; embeddings are the known but deliberately deferred fix, with a richer glossary the cheaper lever until analytics show consistent misses.

nodes.title: Limits
nodes.heading: Secrets live server-side
nodes.summary: The agentic path needs the Anthropic key in the server environment running the endpoint, and the key is never exposed to the browser. If the key is absent at runtime the endpoint serves keyword results, so search degrades rather than breaks.

nodes.title: Limits
nodes.heading: The corpus is your content collection
nodes.summary: hev ask searches only the configured Astro content collections and nothing else, with no crawler, no sitemap ingestion, and no way to index non-collection pages such as hand-written .astro pages. Anything you want searchable must live in a configured collection.

nodes.title: Limits
nodes.heading: The one-shot digest build is bounded; sharded builds are not
nodes.summary: The one-shot build sends the full cleaned corpus to the model in a single call that fits a typical docs site but fails loudly past a size limit rather than degrading. Beyond that, the sharded build splits the corpus into prefix-stable shards each distilled in its own fresh context and merged deterministically, so corpus size stops being a context-window problem even at a very large scale that re-distils only the shard an edit touches; the remaining scale consideration is that the runtime prompt inlines node summaries, so digests with tens of thousands of nodes are not yet a fit for the answer loop.

nodes.title: Quick start
nodes.summary: Walks through adding hev ask to an existing Astro 5 docs site in about five minutes, getting keyword search working first and then adding an Anthropic key to enable agentic answers. The overlay gives keyless instant keyword search that runs anywhere, and a server key turns on the agentic loop on Enter, which you can skip if you do not want agent search.

nodes.title: Quick start
nodes.heading: 1. Install
nodes.summary: Install the package from npm once published, or until then consume it directly from the package subdirectory on GitHub.

nodes.title: Quick start
nodes.heading: 2. Register the integration
nodes.summary: Add the integration to the Astro config with your content collection names and a base path. Collections is the one option you must set; everything else has a default documented in the configuration reference.

nodes.title: Quick start
nodes.heading: 3. Add a server adapter
nodes.summary: The search route renders on demand, so add whichever server or hybrid adapter matches your host while existing pages stay prerendered and only that route runs as a function. The example uses the Cloudflare adapter.

nodes.title: Quick start
nodes.heading: 4. Render the overlay
nodes.summary: Add the overlay component once somewhere global like the base layout, after which any element with the opener attribute opens the palette and the keyboard shortcut is bound automatically. Keyword search then works in dev.

facts.literal:
---
// src/layouts/Base.astro
import SearchOverlay from "@hevmind/ask/components/SearchOverlay.astro";
---
<button type="button" data-hev-ask-open>
  Search <kbd>⌘K</kbd>
</button>

<slot />

<SearchOverlay />

nodes.title: Quick start
nodes.heading: 5. Build the digest
nodes.summary: The digest is an offline-built JSON file you commit that gives the loop domain context, ranks keyword results, supplies the glossary, and holds suggested questions. Build it with the recommended keyless Claude Code skill or the single-call CLI build for CI, then verify anchors and commit; both paths are hash-gated and the integration also builds automatically during astro build when a key is present.

facts.literal:
You: build the hev ask digest

Claude runs:
  ask digest corpus       # emits the sections to distil
  …writes context/glossary/summaries/suggestions…
  ask digest assemble     # writes .hev-ask/digest.json

export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...
pnpm exec ask digest build      # writes .hev-ask/digest.json

pnpm exec ask digest verify     # builds the site, checks every anchor resolves
git add .hev-ask/digest.json

nodes.title: Quick start
nodes.heading: Enable agentic search
nodes.summary: Set the Anthropic key in the server environment where the endpoint runs, after which pressing Enter in the overlay runs the agentic loop with self-issued sub-queries, a grounded answer, and inline deep links. Without a key, Enter returns keyword results.

nodes.title: Quick start
nodes.heading: Prerequisites
nodes.summary: Prerequisites are Astro 5 with at least one content collection, a server or hybrid adapter because the search route renders on demand, and an Anthropic key to enable agentic search. Keyword search needs no key.

nodes.title: Quick start
nodes.heading: Set up keyword search
nodes.summary: Section header introducing the keyword-search setup steps.

nodes.title: Quick start
nodes.heading: Verify it works
nodes.summary: Verify the install by checking that a single heading word deep-links to its section, that a multi-word question or suggestion streams a grounded answer with sub-queries and deep links, and that the anchor verify command exits non-zero on any missing anchor. From there, see the configuration options or Concepts.

nodes.title: Tradeoffs
nodes.summary: Frames the page as the honest account of what hev ask trades away to get what it gives, so readers can decide whether the trade fits their docs.

nodes.title: Tradeoffs
nodes.heading: A committed digest
nodes.summary: Generating the digest offline and committing it as JSON makes it reviewable in pull requests, deterministic, free to read on the request path, and bundled into the edge worker with no runtime filesystem access. The cost is staleness since it only regenerates on content change and a build, but a logged hash-mismatch warning and the cheap idempotent hash gate make rebuilding on every content change in CI the intended workflow.

nodes.title: Tradeoffs
nodes.heading: Cost and latency of agentic search
nodes.summary: The agentic path calls Claude, so it costs small but real money and latency, with worst-case latency roughly the iteration count of Haiku round-trips and the keyword path staying instant as the fast lane. Each query is one bounded loop on the default Haiku model with context prompt-cached, the offline build uses Opus but only when content changes, and keyword-only is a first-class mode for those who want no key in the loop.

nodes.title: Tradeoffs
nodes.heading: How it compares
nodes.summary: Compares hev ask against Pagefind, Algolia DocSearch, and Orama across retrieval, AI ranking, deep links, and hosting. The short version: choose Pagefind for simple keyless static keyword search, Algolia for a managed hosted service with a crawler, Orama for client-side vector search, and hev ask for Astro content collections wanting section deep links and answers that find sections from a reader's question rather than just keywords.

nodes.title: Tradeoffs
nodes.heading: Keyword retrieval, not embeddings
nodes.summary: Retrieval is dependency-free glossary-widened token overlap with no embeddings or vector store, making it self-contained, edge-safe, and instant, with the glossary recovering much of the synonym recall. The cost is a paraphrase-recall ceiling since the agent can only ground in what keyword retrieval surfaced, so for readers who routinely search in non-overlapping words embeddings would do better; that upgrade is deferred, not designed out.

nodes.title: Tradeoffs
nodes.heading: One dependency, deliberately
nodes.summary: hev ask is near zero-dependency with one deliberate exception, the small pure-JS edge-safe github-slugger, chosen because hand-generating anchors risks drifting from Astro's renderer and shipping links that 404. Using the same slugger guarantees byte-identical anchors.

nodes.title: Tradeoffs
nodes.heading: Two paths instead of one
nodes.summary: hev ask runs an instant keyword path and an agentic path and asks the reader to choose by pressing Enter, keeping the common short query instant and keyless while hard questions get a smarter ranker. The cost is a slightly more complex interaction model where readers must learn that Enter means ask AI, judged the right trade for docs where queries split between jumping to a known thing and finding an unnameable one.
